summary: DHH argues that businesses cannot codify every decision into written rules, and attempting to do so would create a constraining straightjacket. Instead, organizations must rely on cultural intuition — the ability to extrapolate from a company's explicit and implicit values to handle novel situations. This capacity develops primarily through osmosis, by observing how decisions are made and internalizing the patterns. When cultural intuition is working well, organizations move with less friction and greater alignment.
key_points: Written rules and decision trees can never fully capture how a business should operate, and over-codifying becomes a straightjacket
Cultural intuition is developed through osmosis — watching decisions, pace, and standards over time and internalizing them
New employees start with an 'empty stomach' for cultural intuition and must build it gradually
When cultural intuition is dialed in, the energy required to go from idea to action to results decreases significantly
money_quotes: We can't write down everything that factors into the conduct of the business.
Such a tome would become a straight jacket sooner than it would serve our drive and progress.
The root of good inference is cultural intuition.
The ability to extrapolate from the explicit and implicit gestalt of the business, and use that to decide how we might tackle the novel and unknown in its spirit.
You watch how decisions are made, how pace is set, how bars are cleared, and you internalize all these factors and more in your gut.
The energy required to get from idea to action to results is less. You're swimming with the stream, in your own way, with your own flair, but in the same direction as the rest of the school.
key_insight: Organizations thrive not by codifying every rule but by cultivating cultural intuition — the shared instinct that lets people make good decisions in novel situations without being told what to do.
